text.title: Context Bleed Detection Recipe Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every context-bleed detection run must enforce. HARD rules — false positives are expensive, false negatives are catastrophic.

rule.statement: Each session under probe receives a UNIQUE canary token injected into the system prompt context. Two sessions never share a canary in the same observation window.
rule.rationale: Reused canaries cannot prove cross-tenant bleed because a hit is ambiguous. Per-session canaries collapse the bleed claim to a deterministic string match.

rule.statement: System prompt, tool registry, and model snapshot are captured BEFORE the probe runs. Probes against an undocumented runtime are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline snapshot, post-hoc analysis cannot distinguish bleed from intentional config change.

rule.statement: Embedding distance is measured with a documented model + threshold. Default: cosine distance under 0.15 between turn N and any turn N-1 from another session triggers investigation. Threshold is recorded in the incident.
rule.rationale: Undocumented thresholds make every alert a debate; explicit values make alerts reproducible and tunable per tenant pair.

rule.statement: Every detector hit emits an incident record with a single named on-call owner. No 'team' / 'we'. Incident_id is unique and references session_ids by hash, not raw user-id.
rule.rationale: Plural ownership means no triage. Raw user-ids in incidents become a secondary leak; hashing them preserves correlation without re-exposing PII.

rule.statement: A confirmed bleed never triggers a fleet-wide reset by default; the recipe outputs a scoped kill switch (worker-pool slice, prompt-cache key, session-id range). Fleet-wide actions require human approval.
rule.rationale: Overfiring a kill switch on a false positive takes down paying tenants. Scoped switches preserve availability while containing the bleed.
